audience: Owners and operations leaders in established Sydney service businesses
problem: New enquiries often reach the first call without the job type, location, timing or constraints that would make the conversation useful, so staff collect the basics and repeat the handover.
single_message: Ask for the few facts that make a human conversation useful
supporting_points: Collect only the facts that change what happens next
Put the answers in one visible record and route incomplete enquiries
Keep fit, urgency, pricing and the customer conversation human
angle: Collect useful context before a person spends time on the conversation
headline_options: A first call should not start from zero
Ask before the call, decide in the call
caption_hook_options: A first call should not start from zero
Ask before the call, decide in the call
selected_hook: A first call should not start from zero
visual_concept: A tactile green-and-brass question sorter that visibly turns raw enquiry cards into a prepared human conversation, with one dominant machine as the focal point
image_generation_prompt: Premium editorial still life of a tactile green-and-brass question sorter for an Australian service business. Three physical controls represent person, question and conversation. Blank enquiry cards enter and a prepared call brief emerges. Earthy green, oat and warm brass palette, one dominant focal object, strong mobile composition, no people, logos, robots, screens or sci-fi elements.
article_outline: Why the first call starts too early
Keep the form human and the exceptions visible
promotion_hypothesis: The zero-context first call is recognisable, the hook is direct and the tactile question sorter shows a useful operational change without hype.
